skill.name: google-calendar-automation
skill.description: Automate Google Calendar scheduling through Rube MCP, including event creation, availability checks, calendar browsing, and attendee updates.
skill.summary: Manage Google Calendar events and availability through Rube MCP workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The numerous external command, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance detections are false positives from Markdown code spans and ordinary calendar guidance. The confirmed risk is that the skill directs agents to use a third-party Rube MCP endpoint and Google OAuth workflows that can access and modify calendar data.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Access to Calendar Data
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add a Rube MCP endpoint and complete Google Calendar OAuth before using calendar tools. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but it routes sensitive calendar metadata and event changes through a third-party integration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MCP endpoint and OAuth setup are explicit, and the documented workflows include creating, updating, deleting, and attendee-management operations. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so this is elevated integration risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Calendar Mutation Workflows Require User Confirmation
medium_findings.description: The skill documents workflows that can create, update, delete, and invite attendees to calendar events. Incorrect use could change a user calendar or notify external attendees without the intended review step.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tool sequences clearly include write operations and attendee notifications. The risk depends on agent behavior and user authorization, so confidence is high for operational risk but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown Code Spans
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backtick-delimited tool names and parameter names as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed file is Markdown guidance and contains no executable script or command substitution evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside Markdown inline code spans and tables describing MCP tool slugs. No shell script, Ruby code, or user-controlled command execution path is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm findings, but the referenced lines describe calendar automation, search query parameters, and attendee patch behavior. No cryptographic API or hashing routine is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural language and calendar parameters, not cryptographic code. This strongly supports a pattern-matching false positive.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported reconnaissance indicators on lines that discuss timezone formats, attendee patch behavior, and long calendar ranges. No host, process, network, or environment enumeration is described.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is scheduling documentation and does not request local system information. No evidence of system reconnaissance was found in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded External MCP Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells users to add a fixed external Rube MCP URL. This is expected for the integration, but marketplace users should understand that calendar operations depend on an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicit and is central to the setup instructions. The risk is integration exposure, not evidence of credential theft.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Calendar Operations Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes delete and replacement update workflows for Google Calendar events. These operations are powerful and should require clear user intent before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented operations are directly named in workflow and quick reference sections. They are normal calendar capabilities but can cause user-visible data changes.

content.value_statement: Scheduling across calendars often fails because event IDs, time zones, and attendee formats are easy to get wrong. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Google Calendar workflows through Rube MCP.
actual_capabilities: Connects agents to Google Calendar workflows through Rube MCP and Composio.
Lists calendars and helps resolve calendar IDs before taking action.
Creates, updates, patches, and deletes Google Calendar events.
Searches events by title, keyword, time range, and recurring instances.
Checks free slots and free-busy periods across selected calendars.
Manages attendee lists and removes attendees by email address.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP connection and authorized Google Calendar access.
Natural language dates are not supported by the documented tools.
Attendees must be email addresses, not display names.
High-volume searches can hit Google Calendar rate limits.

use_cases.title: Book Team Meetings
use_cases.description: Find open slots across calendars, confirm the target calendar, and create a meeting with the right attendees.

use_cases.title: Maintain Event Details
use_cases.description: Search for an existing event, update its time or location, and keep attendee lists accurate.

use_cases.title: Review Calendar Availability
use_cases.description: List events and free-busy periods for a date range before planning projects or travel.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Event
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Google Calendar event on my primary calendar for [date and time] in [IANA timezone]. Title it [event title] and invite [email addresses]. Confirm the details before booking.

prompt_templates.title: Find Available Meeting Times
prompt_templates.prompt: Check free slots for [calendar IDs or primary calendar] between [start timestamp] and [end timestamp]. Use [IANA timezone] and suggest the best [duration] options.

prompt_templates.title: Update an Existing Event
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the event named [event title] between [timeMin] and [timeMax]. Show the matching event details, then update [field changes] only after I confirm the exact event.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multi-Attendee Scheduling
prompt_templates.prompt: Resolve attendee emails for [names], inspect availability across [calendar IDs], propose three meeting options, and create the event only after I choose a slot.

output_examples.input: Schedule a 30 minute product review tomorrow afternoon with two attendees.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for the timezone and attendee email addresses, checks availability, then presents matching time slots for confirmation.

output_examples.input: Move the weekly sync next Tuesday to 10:00 America/New_York.
output_examples.output: The assistant searches for the weekly sync, shows the matching event, and waits for confirmation before patching the start time.

output_examples.input: Find all events with Acme this month.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists matching calendar events with dates, times, calendar names, and pagination status when more results exist.

best_practices: Confirm the calendar, event ID, time, and timezone before any write operation.
Use IANA timezone names and RFC3339 timestamps for searches and free-busy checks.
Preserve existing attendees when patching an event attendee list.
anti_patterns: Do not create or delete events from a vague natural language request without confirmation.
Do not use timezone abbreviations such as EST or PST in tool calls.
Do not patch attendees with only new invitees unless existing attendees should be removed.

faq.question: Does this skill require Google OAuth?
faq.answer: Yes. The user must connect Google Calendar through Rube MCP before calendar tools can run.

faq.question: Can it create Google Calendar events?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents event creation after the target calendar, start time, timezone, title, and attendees are known.

faq.question: Can it find free meeting slots?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Google Calendar availability and free-busy workflows for selected calendars and time ranges.

faq.question: Does it accept natural language dates?
faq.answer: No. The documented tools require ISO 8601 or RFC3339 timestamps with timezone information.

faq.question: Can it manage attendees?
faq.answer: Yes. It can add or remove attendees, but attendee values must be email addresses.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The skill depends on a third-party MCP endpoint with access to authorized calendar data and event write operations.
